There will be 4 hours of lectures and 1 hour tutorial/computer lab class per week. Tutorial/lab classes provide an opportunity to develop analytical skills by practice on provided problems.
This course is intended as an elective for Statistics majors & minors, Quantitative Data Science Major and Mathematics majors, both at the undergraduate and postgraduate level. Prior knowledge of basic probability is necessary, and knowledge of stochastic processes will be advantageous but is not required. This course complements MATH3191 Optimisation for data science by focusing on various neural network architectures and training methods that make use of some optimisation methods explored in MATH3191, along with probabilistic aspects governing generative modelling. Additionally, this course focuses on mathematical and statistical foundations of many machine learning methods discussed from a computational perspective in MATH3856. This course is jointly taught at two levels. MATH3881 is for 3rd year undergraduates, with MATH5881 for Honours & Masters students.
enrolmentConditions: Pre-requisites: MATH2801 or MATH2901

title: Higher Probability and Stochastic Processes
subject: MATH
description: This is an advanced level course in probability theory and stochastic processes for mathematics/statistics and data science students. Topics covered include rigorous definition of probability and random variables; convergence of random variables; Poisson and Wiener process, and Ito Calculus. This version of the course will require knowledge of more proofs and technical arguments than MATH3801 Probability and Stochastic Processes.
enrolmentConditions: Prerequisite: (MATH2901 or MATH2801(DN)) and (MATH2501 or MATH2601) and (MATH2011 or MATH2111)

title: Higher Statistical Inference
subject: MATH
description:
This course introduces students to the field of statistical inference including the main parametric and non-parametric techniques. During seminars and tutorials a range of topics will be introduced including Uniformly minimum variance estimation, Cramer-Rao inequality, Lehmann-Scheffe theorem, Monotone likelihood ratio distributions and uniformly most powerful unbiased tests, Generalised likelihood ratio tests, exact tests and large sample tests, Bayesian point estimation, interval estimation and hypothesis testing, Robustness and bootstrap resampling, oOrder statistics, goodness of fit, contingency tables, Statistical inference based on ranks, Oone sample, two sample and k-sample problems, blocked data, independence and association.
This course is the higher version of MATH3811, with topics explored at greater depth.
enrolmentConditions: Prerequisite: MATH2931 or MATH2831(DN).

title: Categorical Data Analysis
subject: MATH
description:
This undergraduate course provides the theoretical foundation and methodological background for the analysis of categorical response data. Through a series of lectures and labs, a range of topics will be introduced including the Bernoulli trial, the analysis of contingency tables, and generalised linear models including logistic and Poisson regression. Statistical computing is an integral part of this course.
This course is jointly taught at two levels: MATH3945 is for 3rd year undergraduate students and MATH5945 for Honours & Masters students. Lectures will be conducted simultaneously for both streams, but tutorial classes and computer labs will be conducted separately for the two groups.
enrolmentConditions: No conditions for enrolment found on Handbook page.

title: Mathematics and Statistics Honours Thesis
subject: MATH
description:
This course is only available to students enrolled in an Honours Program in the School of Mathematics and Statistics and enrolment must be approved by the Schoolâs Honours Coordinator.
This course is taken three times over three consecutive terms (3 x 6 UoC for a total of 18 UoC). It forms the research thesis project component of the honours streams for programs within the School of Mathematics and Statistics. The three instances of the course form part of the one thesis project. The student will undertake independent study in the appropriate area of applied mathematics, pure mathematics, physical oceanography, or statistics according to their honours stream. The student will be supervised by at least one member of the academic staff of the School of Mathematics and Statistics, possibly jointly with an academic external to the School, or, with the permission of the Head of School, a suitable other person in a non-university research position. The student will write a thesis, give an oral seminar presentation on the thesis to the appropriate department, and attend an oral assessment ( viva voce) with examiners to elaborate on their thesis topic.
The final thesis will be assessed on four major areas: exposition, literature coverage, critical analysis and insight originality. The weighting of these components will depend on the type of thesis which is determined by discussion between the student and supervisor(s) when the topic is chosen. All theses are expected to address all four areas.
Regular honours meetings will be timetabled as part of the thesis courses and participation will be assessed. The main purpose of these meetings is to provide a forum for honours students to give an oral presentation of their work to, and receive feedback from their peers and the honours coordinators. The meetings will also be used for other honours training purposes on more general mathematical and statistical skills, such as literature, research methodology, writing, presentation, or AI skills.
enrolmentConditions: Prerequisite: Enrolled in MATH Honours stream

title: Biomathematics
subject: MATH
description: This course introduces mathematical modelling and data analysis for biological and biomedical systems. The course is suitable for students with an interest in the real-world application of differential equation modelling. Examples of the biological systems studied in the course include: population dynamics, the spread of infectious diseases, drug delivery and metabolism, biochemical reactions, the formation of animal coat patterns, and the growth of tumours. The emphasis in this course is on the development of the governing model equations, and on the analysis and interpretation of the model solutions. Methods for computer simulation of the model equations will be demonstrated but studentsâ knowledge of these methods will not be assessed in the course. In addition to lectures, the course will feature tutorials and workshops. Weekly tutorials require the class to solve a series of mathematical problems that build upon the lecture material. Workshops (several during the course) further consolidate the lecture material as the class makes use of modelling, analysis and computer simulations to perform in-depth studies of particular biological applications. Assumed Knowledge: Completion of two years of an undergraduate degree in mathematics or statistics.
enrolmentConditions: Prerequisite: Enrolment in program 5659 or 8161 or 8719 or 8750 or 7659
